merged_events.event_description: Diplomatic relations established when U.S. Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary John B. Jackson presented credentials to Prince Nikola. Jackson and successors resided in Athens, Greece, making only annual visits to Cetinje.

evidence.quote: Diplomatic relations were established on October 30, 1905, when John B. Jackson, U.S. Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary, presented his credentials to Prince Nikola. Jackson and his successors resided in Athens, Greece and made annual visits to Cetinje, the Montenegrin capitol.

merged_events.event_description: Diplomatic relations ended when President Woodrow Wilson withdrew recognition of Montenegro as an independent state.

evidence.quote: Diplomatic relations ended on December 30, 1920, when President Woodrow Wilson withdrew recognition of Montenegro as an independent state (see Recognitions above).

merged_events.event_description: Diplomatic relations re-established via exchange of letters between President Bush and Montenegrin President Vujanovic. A U.S. Consulate was already present in Podgorica (the capital), which would later be elevated to an embassy.

evidence.quote: Diplomatic relations were established on August 15, 2006, with an exchange of letters between President George W. Bush and Montenegrin President Filip Vujanovic.
The American Embasy in Podgorica was established on October 5, 2006, when the U.S. Consulate became an embassy, with Arlene Ferrill as Charge d'Affaires ad interim.

merged_events.event_description: The American Embassy in Podgorica was established when the U.S. Consulate was elevated to embassy status, with Arlene Ferrill as Chargé d'Affaires ad interim.

evidence.quote: The American Embasy in Podgorica was established on October 5, 2006, when the U.S. Consulate became an embassy, with Arlene Ferrill as Charge d'Affaires ad interim.

merged_events.event_description: Montenegro was absorbed into the Kingdom of the Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes, ending U.S. diplomatic representation to Montenegro.

evidence.quote: Montenegro was absorbed into the Kingdom of the Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes on 1918-12-04.

matched.notes: Exact match on date (1905-10-30) and status (Envoy Nonresident). Diplomatic relations established when John B. Jackson presented credentials.

CSV date (1920-12-30) matches RDCR Event 1 exactly. POCOM Event 4 gives 1918-12-04 (Montenegro absorbed into Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes), but RDCR explicitly states Wilson withdrew recognition on 1920-12-30, so RDCR wins per source priority. The 1918 date is a related historical fact, not the formal end of U.S. diplomatic relations.
Exact match on date (2006-10-05) and status (Embassy). U.S. Consulate in Podgorica elevated to Embassy.

missing_from_csv.event_description: Diplomatic relations re-established via exchange of letters between President Bush and Montenegrin President Vujanovic. RDCR indicates a U.S. Consulate was already present in Podgorica, which was later elevated to an Embassy on 2006-10-05. The re-establishment of relations on August 15, 2006 with a Consulate already in place suggests a Consulate status existed before the Embassy elevation.
missing_from_csv.notes: Medium confidence extraction. RDCR line 34 states relations were established on August 15, 2006, and line 38 states the Consulate became an Embassy on October 5, 2006. This implies a Consulate status period from 2006-08-15 to 2006-10-05. The CSV jumps directly to Embassy on 2006-10-05, omitting this intermediate Consulate status. Candidate for addition.
